retrieved_docs.content: Central precocious puberty

retrieved_docs.title: Precocious Puberty -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: The decision to treat depends on the age of the child and the progression of puberty. If the child has rapidly progressing symptoms or if bone age is significantly advanced, consider treatment. The main goals of treatment are to preserve the adult height and to alleviate the associated psychosocial stress. GnRH agonists are the standard of care. [6] Many different formulations (intranasal, intramuscular and subcutaneous) of long and short-acting GnRH agonists exist. The choice of the formulation depends on the patient and clinician preference.  In the United States, leuprolide acetate is the most common. It is a depot injection administered every 3 months.

retrieved_docs.title: Precocious Puberty -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Treatment is directed towards eliminating the source of sex steroids. Surgery is indicated in gonadal and adrenal tumors.  If exogenous sources of sex steroids are identified, they should be eliminated. Classic congenital CAH is treated with glucocorticoids. In McCune-Albright syndrome, some benefit occurs with blocking the estrogen synthesis using aromatase inhibitors (anastrozole, letrozole) and selective estrogen selective receptor modulator (tamoxifen).  The optimal treatment for familial male-limited precocious puberty is not well established, but the preferred treatment is a combination of an androgen antagonist (spironolactone) and an aromatase inhibitor (anastrozole, testolactone). [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Tamoxifen -- Indications -- Off-Label Uses
retrieved_docs.content: Precocious puberty due to McCune-Albright syndrome in females. [20]

retrieved_docs.title: Peripheral Precocious Puberty -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Familial male-limited precocious puberty: This disorder is treated with an androgen receptor antagonist and aromatase inhibitor to delay epiphyseal maturation. A GnRH analog is added in cases where central precocious puberty occurs.  A case series of 25 subjects with familial male-limited precocious puberty showed that spironolactone with testolactone or anastrozole and a GnRH agonist was effective in increasing adult height. [40] Other drugs used to manage this condition include ketoconazole, which is a steroid synthesis inhibitor, and bicalutamide, which is an anti-androgen and letrozole.

retrieved_docs.title: Peripheral Precocious Puberty -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Primary hypothyroidism is treated by providing thyroxine supplementation.

retrieved_docs.title: Leuprolide -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: In treating children with central precocious puberty with leuprolide, the goal is to slow down the progression of the secondary sex characteristics until an age when they are appropriate. If left untreated, these patients typically have diminished adult height. With the use of leuprolide, the predicted adult height increases because it slows down the closing of the growth plates. Leuprolide discontinuation should merit consideration when at the appropriate time for puberty. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Peripheral Precocious Puberty -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Cases of peripheral precocious puberty should be promptly referred to an endocrinologist to prevent complications. The goals of managing peripheral precocious puberty are to treat the underlying cause and block the production or response of excess sex hormones. Specific treatments depend on the cause:

Outline the treatment and management options available for precocious puberty. Summarize interprofessional team strategies for improving care coordination and communication to improve outcomes in children with precocious puberty. retrieved_docs.title: Delayed Puberty -- Treatment / Management -- Constitutional Delay in Puberty and Growth
retrieved_docs.content: After making a diagnosis of CDPG, treatment is usually guided by patient and parental goals. Close observation can be appropriate, especially if puberty has started and adulthood stature is not of primary concern. A short-course treatment with low doses of testosterone for males or estrogen for females is often initiated when puberty and growth are true psychosocial causes of stress and low self-esteem for a child. History of bullying, poor academic performance, or dropping out of athletics may lead the provider to start treatment. Treatment can improve growth velocity, sexual maturation, and mental well-being without producing serious side effects, or significantly affecting the final target height. [14] For males with CDPD, an oral and intramuscular (IM) form of testosterone are available. However, the IM form is usually more appropriate due to the serious side effect of liver toxicity caused by oral testosterone. In females with CDPG, an oral and IM form of estrogen are also available, but oral estrogen is more often the therapeutic choice. Once treatment has started, the patient must be monitored frequently for signs of pubertal development, which includes testicular enlargement in males or breast development in females. After a few months of therapy, the provider may choose to stop sex-steroid treatment to evaluate for continued pubertal development while off therapy. [3] For patients that are more concerned with short stature than with delayed puberty, growth hormone therapy has been used to augment height. However, it has not been shown to truly impact final adult height in adolescents with CDPG, and is thus, not recommended for treatment. [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Menstrual Suppression -- Contraindications -- Age-Related Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: Except for care for transgender youth, menstrual suppression should not be initiated before menarche; adolescents must first demonstrate normal anatomy and typical pubertal development. [27] [29]

retrieved_docs.title: Gender Dysphoria -- Treatment / Management -- Hormonal Therapy
retrieved_docs.content: Providers in this area of care are encouraged to start hormonal therapy before puberty for maximum results and patient satisfaction. However, parental consent for children and young adolescents for initiating hormonal therapy remains controversial. The physician should inform the patients that testosterone is teratogenic. Contraceptive methods, including IUDs, etc., should be discussed with the patients. [10] Surgical Therapy

retrieved_docs.title: Peripheral Precocious Puberty -- History and Physical -- History
retrieved_docs.content: Patients should be inquired about the ingestion of sex hormones or exposure to topical agents with estrogenic/androgenic/endocrine-disrupting activity.

retrieved_docs.title: Dwarfism -- Treatment / Management -- Hormonal Therapy
retrieved_docs.content: Gonadotropin-releasing hormone (GnRH) analogs are used to halt the progression of precocious puberty. When administered, these analogs continuously suppress the release of gonadotropins from the pituitary and further down the gonadal steroids, which were responsible for premature bone maturation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 2279
retrieved_docs.content: Luteinizing Hormone‑releasing Hormone Agonist is classified as a hormone and related agent.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 1368
retrieved_docs.content: Sex hormones and modulators of the genital system constitute a therapeutic class that encompasses both gonadotropins and other ovulation stimulants and combination formulations of progestogens and estrogens. Within this class, progestogens and estrogens in combination represent a distinct subgroup, and fixed combinations of progestogens and estrogens are a specific type of that subgroup.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 1367
retrieved_docs.content: Androgens and estrogens and Androgens and female sex hormones in combination with other drugs are both classified as types of androgens and female sex hormones in combination, indicating that therapeutic regimens that combine androgenic agents with female sex hormones can be administered either as a straightforward androgen‑estrogen mixture or as a more complex formulation that also includes additional pharmacologic agents. Recognizing these subcategories helps clinicians tailor hormone therapy to achieve the desired hormonal balance while addressing the need for adjunctive drugs in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 2 -- Community 745
retrieved_docs.content: Androgens and estrogens represent a combined formulation of male (androgen) and female (estrogen) sex hormones; this combination is classified as a member of the broader “estrogens / testosterone” group and is defined as an instance of “androgens and female sex hormones in combination.” A more complex preparation that adds other drugs to this mixture is also considered a subtype of the same “androgens and female sex hormones in combination” category. The overarching class “SEX HORMONES AND MODULATORS OF THE GENITAL SYSTEM” includes three major subgroups: hormonal contraceptives for systemic use, the basic combination of androgens and female sex hormones, and gonadotropins together with other ovulation‑stimulating agents. Within this hierarchy, hormonal contraceptives for systemic use are identified as a specific type of emergency contraception (pohotovotní antikoncepce). Together, these relationships delineate how various hormone‑based therapies and contraceptive agents are organized within the broader context of sex‑hormone pharmacology and genital system modulation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2065
retrieved_docs.content: Androgens and female sex hormones in combination with other drugs are classified as a specific type of androgens and female sex hormones in combination.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1761
retrieved_docs.content: Progestogens, endocrine therapy drugs are classified as a type of hormones and related agents.

retrieved_docs.content: pharmacologic class, mechanism of action, and therapeutic context of each agent, particularly when selecting combination hormone products like ESTROGENS/MEDROXYPROGESTERONE for hormone replacement or contraceptive purposes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 1 -- Community 149
retrieved_docs.content: sex‑hormone framework. Collectively, these relationships delineate a comprehensive map of androgen‑estrogen combinations, multiple progestogen subclasses, their contraceptive and endocrine‑therapy formulations, receptor‑modulating agents, and associated biomarker concepts, providing a structured basis for clinical classification, therapeutic decision‑making, and regulatory categorization.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2648
retrieved_docs.content: Progestogens and estrogens, fixed combinations are a specific type of progestogens and estrogens in combination, representing a subclass of combined hormone therapy in which the progestogen and estrogen components are formulated together in predetermined, unchanging doses.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 2018
retrieved_docs.content: Hormonal contraceptives for systemic use are classified as a form of pohotovostní antikoncepce, meaning that systemically administered hormonal methods serve as emergency contraception.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1276
retrieved_docs.content: Sex hormones and modulators of the genital system represent a broad therapeutic class that includes both hormonal contraceptives for systemic use and gonadotropins and other ovulation stimulants as specific subcategories. Within this framework, hormonal contraceptives for systemic use are identified as a form of emergency contraception (pohotovostní antikoncepce). This hierarchical organization highlights how agents that alter reproductive hormone activity—ranging from systemic contraceptive formulations to ovulation‑stimulating gonadotropins—are grouped under the overarching category of sex hormones and genital system modulators, facilitating a clearer understanding of their roles in reproductive health management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 1 -- Community 248
retrieved_docs.content: drugs. Sequential systemic contraceptive preparations, including emergency contraception, are grouped under hormonal contraceptives for systemic use. This hierarchical framework links agents by chemical structure, therapeutic indication (contraception, endocrine therapy, receptor modulation), and formulation type, providing a coherent reference for prescribing, research, and pharmacological categorization.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 1369
retrieved_docs.content: Medroxyprogesterone and megestrol are both classified as progestogen systemic hormonal contraceptives, meaning they are progestogen‑based agents administered systemically for contraceptive purposes. As members of this drug class, they share the common mechanism of providing hormonal contraception through progestogenic activity.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 1442
retrieved_docs.content: Progestogens used as endocrine therapy drugs are classified as hormones and related agents, and the drug megestrol is included as a member of this progestogen class.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 4 -- Community 2746
retrieved_docs.content: Progestogen systemic hormonal contraceptives constitute a class of systemic birth‑control agents that rely on synthetic progestogen compounds, and norethindrone is a specific progestogen that belongs to this class, illustrating the pharmacologic composition of these contraceptive methods.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 2 -- Community 851
retrieved_docs.content: Progestogens and estrogens systemic contraceptives, sequential preparations are classified as hormonal contraceptives for systemic use, which in turn belong to the broader category of pohotovotní antikoncepce (emergency contraception). These sequential preparations specifically comprise the dienogest / estradiol combination, indicating that they are members of the dienogest / estradiol formulation group. Recognizing this hierarchy helps clinicians understand that sequential progestogen‑estrogen regimens are systemic hormonal methods that can be employed as emergency contraception and that their active components are the hormones dienogest and estradiol.
